chunk_summary: Feeding plans for non-breastfed children aged 6–24 months focus on ensuring adequate nutrition, hygiene, and growth monitoring. Recommendations include specific feeding frequencies, milk intake limits, and nutrient-dense complementary foods to address infection risks and micronutrient gaps.
learning_objectives: Understand feeding frequency and dietary composition for non-breastfed children aged 6–24 months.
Identify appropriate milk sources and their limitations to prevent iron deficiency and anemia.
Learn strategies to ensure hygiene and safe hydration for non-breastfed infants.
Recognize the importance of growth monitoring and supplementation in vulnerable children.
key_facts: Non-breastfed children aged 6–11 months require 4–5 feeding episodes daily, including 3 solid/semi-solid meals and 1–2 milk feeds.
Milk intake should not exceed 500 mL/day to prevent iron deficiency; animal milk must be boiled or pasteurized.
From 12–24 months, provide 3 family meals, 2 nutritious snacks, and 250–500 mL of milk daily.
Energy-dense meals should include added oil/ghee and an iron-rich item (e.g., fortified cereal, egg, meat, or pulses with vitamin C).
Use a cup instead of a bottle to reduce dental caries and contamination risks.
Monitor growth monthly for children under 12 months or those with suboptimal weight gain.
reference_quote: Non-breastfed infants and toddlers have higher vulnerability to infection and micronutrient gaps because they do not receive the anti-infective and nutrient contributions of breast milk. — Guiding principles for feeding non-breastfed children 6–24 months of age, WHO
document_key_topics: Feeding frequency and dietary composition for non-breastfed children
Milk intake guidelines and iron deficiency prevention
Growth monitoring and supplementation for vulnerable children
